attribution.name: Elevation of Privilege
attribution.authors: Adam Shostack / Fraser Scott, Microsoft Corporation

cards.title: Spoofing 2
cards.description: An attacker could take over the port or socket that the server normally uses

cards.title: Spoofing 3
cards.description: An attacker could try one credential after another and there's nothing to slow them down (online or offline)

cards.title: Spoofing 4
cards.description: An attacker can anonymously connect, because we expect authentication to be done at a higher level

cards.title: Spoofing 5
cards.description: An attacker can confuse a client because there are too many ways to identify a server

cards.title: Spoofing 6
cards.description: An attacker can spoof a server because identifiers aren't stored on the client and checked for consistency on re-connection (that is, there's no key persistence)

cards.title: Spoofing 7
cards.description: An attacker can connect to a server or peer over a link that isn't authenticated (and encrypted)

cards.title: Spoofing 8
cards.description: An attacker could steal credentials stored on the server and reuse them (for example, a key is stored in a world readable file)

cards.title: Spoofing 9
cards.description: An attacker who gets a password can reuse it (Use stronger authenticators)

cards.title: Spoofing 10
cards.description: An attacker can choose to use weaker or no authentication

cards.title: Spoofing J
cards.description: An attacker could steal credentials stored on the client and reuse them

cards.title: Spoofing Q
cards.description: An attacker could go after the way credentials are updated or recovered (account recovery doesn't require disclosing the old password)

cards.title: Spoofing K
cards.description: Your system ships with a default admin password, and doesn't force a change

cards.title: Spoofing A
cards.description: You've invented a new Spoofing attack

cards.title: Tampering 2
cards.description: An attacker can modify your build system and produce signed builds of your software

cards.title: Tampering 3
cards.description: An attacker can take advantage of your custom key exchange or integrity control which you built instead of using standard crypto

cards.title: Tampering 4
cards.description: Your code makes access control decisions all over the place, rather than with a security kernel

cards.title: Tampering 5
cards.description: An attacker can replay data without detection because your code doesn't provide timestamps or sequence numbers

cards.title: Tampering 6
cards.description: An attacker can write to a data store your code relies on

cards.title: Tampering 7
cards.description: An attacker can bypass permissions because you don't make names canonical before checking access permissions

cards.title: Tampering 8
cards.description: An attacker can manipulate data because there's no integrity protection for data on the network

cards.title: Tampering 9
cards.description: An attacker can provide or control state information

cards.title: Tampering 10
cards.description: An attacker can alter information in a data store because it has weak/open permissions or includes a group which is equivalent to everyone ("anyone with a Facebook account")

cards.title: Tampering J
cards.description: An attacker can write to some resource because permissions are granted to the world or there are no ACLs

cards.title: Tampering Q
cards.description: An attacker can change parameters over a trust boundary and after validation (for example, important parameters in a hidden field in HTML, or passing a pointer to critical memory)

cards.title: Tampering K
cards.description: An attacker can load code inside your process via an extension point

cards.title: Tampering A
cards.description: You've invented a new Tampering attack

cards.title: Repudiation 2
cards.description: An attacker can pass data through the log to attack a log reader, and there's no documentation of what sorts of validation are done

cards.title: Repudiation 3
cards.description: A low privilege attacker can read interesting security information in the logs

cards.title: Repudiation 4
cards.description: An attacker can alter digital signatures because the digital signature system you're implementing is weak, or uses MACs where it should use a signature

cards.title: Repudiation 5
cards.description: An attacker can alter log messages on a network because they lack strong integrity controls

cards.title: Repudiation 6
cards.description: An attacker can create a log entry without a timestamp (or no log entry is timestamped)

cards.title: Repudiation 7
cards.description: An attacker can make the logs wrap around and lose data

cards.title: Repudiation 8
cards.description: An attacker can make a log lose or confuse security information

cards.title: Repudiation 9
cards.description: An attacker can use a shared key to authenticate as different principals, confusing the information in the logs

cards.title: Repudiation 10
cards.description: An attacker can get arbitrary data into logs from unauthenticated (or weakly authenticated) outsiders without validation

cards.title: Repudiation J
cards.description: An attacker can edit logs and there's no way to tell (perhaps because there's no heartbeat option for the logging system)

cards.title: Repudiation Q
cards.description: An attacker can say "I didn't do that," and you'd have no way to prove them wrong

cards.title: Repudiation K
cards.description: The system has no logs

cards.title: Repudiation A
cards.description: You've invented a new Repudiation attack

cards.title: Information Disclosure 2
cards.description: An attacker can brute-force file encryption because there's no defense in place (example defense, password stretching)

cards.title: Information Disclosure 3
cards.description: An attacker can see error messages with security sensitive content

cards.title: Information Disclosure 4
cards.description: An attacker can read content because messages (say, an email or HTTP cookie) aren't encrypted even if the channel is encrypted

cards.title: Information Disclosure 5
cards.description: An attacker may be able to read a document or data because it's encrypted with a non-standard algorithm

cards.title: Information Disclosure 6
cards.description: An attacker can read data because it's hidden or occluded (for undo or change tracking) and the user might forget that it's there

cards.title: Information Disclosure 7
cards.description: An attacker can act as a 'man in the middle' because you don't authenticate endpoints of a network connection

cards.title: Information Disclosure 8
cards.description: An attacker can access information through a search indexer, logger, or other such mechanism

cards.title: Information Disclosure 9
cards.description: An attacker can read sensitive information in a file with permissive permissions

cards.title: Information Disclosure 10
cards.description: An attacker can read information in files or databases with no access controls

cards.title: Information Disclosure J
cards.description: An attacker can discover the fixed key being used to encrypt

cards.title: Information Disclosure Q
cards.description: An attacker can read the entire channel because the channel (say, HTTP or SMTP) isn't encrypted

cards.title: Information Disclosure K
cards.description: An attacker can read network information because there's no cryptography used

cards.title: Information Disclosure A
cards.description: You've invented a new Information Disclosure attack

cards.title: Denial of Service 2
cards.description: An attacker can make your authentication system unusable or unavailable

cards.title: Denial of Service 3
cards.description: An attacker can drain our easily replacable battery (battery, temporary)

cards.title: Denial of Service 4
cards.description: An attacker can drain a battery that's hard to replace (sealed in a phone, an implanted medical device, or in a hard to reach location) (battery, persist)

cards.title: Denial of Service 5
cards.description: An attacker can spend our cloud budget (budget, persist)

cards.title: Denial of Service 6
cards.description: An attacker can make a server unavailable or unusable without ever authenticating but the problem goes away when the attacker stops (server, anonymous, temporary)

cards.title: Denial of Service 7
cards.description: An attacker can make a client unavailable or unusable and the problem persists after the attacker goes away (client, auth, persist)

cards.title: Denial of Service 8
cards.description: An attacker can make a server unavailable or unusable and the problem persists after the attacker goes away (server, auth, persist)

cards.title: Denial of Service 9
cards.description: An attacker can make a client unavailable or unusable without ever authenticating and the problem persists after the attacker goes away (client, anon, persist)

cards.title: Denial of Service 10
cards.description: An attacker can make a server unavailable or unusable without ever authenticating and the problem persists after the attacker goes away (server, anon, persist)

cards.title: Denial of Service J
cards.description: An attacker can cause the logging subsystem to stop working

cards.title: Denial of Service Q
cards.description: An attacker can amplify a Denial of Service attack through this component with amplification on the order of 10 to 1

cards.title: Denial of Service K
cards.description: An attacker can amplify a Denial of Service attack through this component with amplification on the order of 100 to 1

cards.title: Denial of Service A
cards.description: You've invented a new Denial of Service attack

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 2
cards.description: An attacker has compromised a key technology supplier

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 3
cards.description: An attacker can access the cloud service which manages your devices

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 4
cards.description: An attacker can escape from a container or other sandbox

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 5
cards.description: An attacker can force data through different validation paths which give different results

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 6
cards.description: An attacker could take advantage of permissions you set, but don't use

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 7
cards.description: An attacker can provide a pointer across a trust boundary, rather than data which can be validated

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 8
cards.description: An attacker can enter data that is checked while still under their control and used later on the other side of a trust boundary

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 9
cards.description: There's no reasonable way for a caller to figure out what validation of tainted data you perform before passing it to them

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege 10
cards.description: There's no reasonable way for a caller to figure out what security assumptions you make

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege J
cards.description: An attacker can reflect input back to a user, like cross site scripting

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege Q
cards.description: You include user-generated content within your page, possibly including the content of random URLs

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege K
cards.description: An attacker can inject a command that the system will run at a higher privilege level

cards.title: Elevation of Privilege A
cards.description: You've invented a new Elevation of Privilege attack
